item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The edit note for the revision that introduced that line reads, "Site-5 does not exist, there has never been a Site-5." and the original link was to SCP-2956. The link was changed the next month to the page url "SCP-Err0r".

I think it's most likely intentional, but you'd need to confirm with the original author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, the broken link in the following:

Personnel are to be reminded that there is no Site-5.

is intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor editing consideration: the fourth footnote, "To date, the officer's firearms have been returned without a single discharged round," could be read two ways. Either the firearms are returned without any rounds having been discharged, or they are returned with a single, [presumably discharged] round missing. From the content of the article, I'd guess that you mean the former, but it could also imply an additional mysterious phenomenon, in the form of dissappearing bullets not actually used in the dispatch of the D-Class.

item.title: Good job!
item.content:encoded: This article has good writing, a good balance of weirdness, and has made my headcanon. It's an interesting take on the mystery and, in my opinion, stupidity of executing d-class. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't speak for Golden, but I think the whole idea of the Class-D hovering, then suddenly "shooting" up into the sky goes beyond one's suspension of disbelief. Maybe if before that part you write that the clearing is unnaturally quiet (no birdsong, no breeze, etc.), and that right before the Class-D are abducted a strange "whirring" sound is picked up by Agent Ysson's cam. Without something indicating to the reader what might come next (alien abduction), it kind of comes off as the D-class being "magical" or something silly like that.

Maybe if you could sprinkle hints here and there in the main article that aliens are somehow involved, but the reader doesn't realize all the clues are there until the whole "alien abduction" thing at the end is revealed, it would make a big difference. Also, it might be helpful if you give some indication as to why Agent Ysson is abducted as well. You want to keep it mysterious and make the reader keep wondering, but I think… Well, you know http://www.scp-wiki.net/taboo ? The thing that makes it so great is that the answers are all there as to why the entities periodically "transform", why the place must be left "unnameable", why the iron levels of personnel who break the rules regarding this SCP are unusually low. It isn't until we see the anomaly in action right at the end that we realize that seemingly random or "generic" details mentioned before are actually crucial in understanding just what is happening in this peculiar forest.

Overall, I really like the idea that the monthly termination is memetic in nature. It always bothered me that the Foundation would just "dispose of" all the D-class. I mean, a lot of the D-class are horrible people, but a lot of them were in prison for more mundane reasons such as card counting, simple assault, robbery, etc. Heck, there's even that D-class personnel with Down syndrome who is currently the only "thing" containing that Keter-class teddy bear. Clearly, not all D-class are prisoners who are serving life sentences or have received the death penalty. It would be a jerk move on the part of the Foundation to go around killing D-class who were in prison for something as mundane as grand theft auto. Plus, there's plenty of D-class personnel who are good at their jobs and are cooperative with Research staff. It would be a waste to kill them and have to train new D-class every month.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending is kinda integral, so it's not going anywhere. In what way did it ruin the article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was pretty good, but it was absolutely ruined by the ending. In my opinion it would of been better if you had just cut out the last addendum altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know that Foundation is a huge organization, but they don't simply got Class-D from everyone.

unless,
maybe,
they do

Also, it is unethical, and Ethical Comitee would probably dislike it.

Depends on the axioms you're working from. If the risk to the population from contamination or other hidden long-term effect gets severe and probable enough in relation to the definite loss of life from monthly terminations, it could become unethical not to do them. I don't headcanon monthly terminations as a Foundation-wide thing because there are so many more realistic alternatives, but there are all kinds of scenarios that could justify it for a specific anomaly or site. I like to imagine that in articles where they do it everywhere, things are so bad that the Ethics Committee has already determined it to be the best option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people's heads are unpleasant. What can you do?

Mine, for example, contains the knowledge that the Tuskegee expirements and MKUltra were things and that "prisoners awaiting execution" includes thousands of citizens of the PRC each year as well as people in places like Saudi Arabia who have been convicted of "crimes" like Witchcraft and Sorcery that are ordinary civil behavior elsewhere in the world. You could also include the tens of thousands of people who will be killed this year in the DPRK's re-education system, who may not be under an official death sentence, but are still being killed by the state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice skip. Come on, guys. If monthly termination of Class-D is a headcanon, it would be kind of unpleasing. I know that Foundation is a huge organization, but they don't simply got Class-D from everyone. Also, it is unethical, and Ethical Comitee would probably dislike it.

Very nicely done. Very nice. This is a very wonderful example of "headcanon incarnate", and while I thought the event log was just going to be rehashing some of the earlier stuff, it turned the entire piece on its head in the best way possible.

Well deserved +200, and thank you for the new headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think monthly termination really did exist when the Foundation was smaller. And if ALL the D-class were death row inmates, itd be their responsibility to execute these people on time. In fact, I thought maybe their execution was PUSHED BACK one month for the sake of helping out with SCPs.

Anyway, there are way too many anomalies now. D-class are more valuable than they would've been before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the sort of thing that made sense earlier on as well. Think of it this way. If the SCP Foundation was a small number of people just doing their thing, trying to keep locked up a few weird little dealies they found, but they did have a LOT of resources, they might be able to get a few people a month who would be otherwise executed/whatever. It seemed like it was an operation at first that was, at the largest, the size of something like Google or Amazon. It seemed like there was likely about 10-100 D-Class, maybe two or three sites. From how all the crossposting was done, it seemed like any SCP could readily be combined with most any other SCP.

And it passes the 'smell' test. Super covert operation. Has to get test subjects from somewhere. Doesn't care if they live or die. Well, they probably kill them, don't they? After all, part of the narrative conceit of (most) SCP articles is that the Foundation exists within the world we live in. Any day now, you could grab a cup of coffee from Starbucks and it'll steal your identity. So since there's not a lot of death row people we here getting out, they're likely dead. It's only once you start thinking practically "Wait a minute, how many D-Class are there…?" That you start to realize that this is actually ridiculous.

I would also say it makes the thing a little LESS edgy in a lot of cases. After all. The guys die every time, but that becomes less awful when we think "Well, they'd die anyway…" And the fact that, what are we going to do, let them go? They're often serial killers. Is the foundation really going to permit a serial killer to go free on the grounds that he or she was lucky enough to be assigned to wearing gloves that turn your fingers into snuggly ball pythons instead of "Let's see what happens if we put THIS one in the room with the murderdemon…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm perfectly fine with retconning it because it never made sense to me as it always felt like a monumental waste of a limited resource.

item.title: Re: this is the stupidest thing ever
item.content:encoded: 'stupidest' isn't even a word I don't think you're qualified to call something stupid

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So you’re saying it’s easier to kill the D-class(in some ways yes it is) and dispose the body/remains,than to mindwipe them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I think in a lot of the old articles it's kind of just to be edgy. There's some in-universe justification, in that you would want to avoid using the same test subject on too many skips, because hidden long-term effects from earlier tests might contaminate your later results, and going to the trouble of amnesticizing and releasing them when you're done is way harder than just shooting them and calling it a day. But, mostly, I think it just adds an extra layer of darkness and "wtf" to the setting. I appreciated it as a new reader, but as time went by I came to think it doesn't make sense for all the reasons discussed upthread, so I'm glad there's articles like this to play around with the trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also why kill the D-class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This website is like a hole,the deeper you go down,the darker it gets.
Then you don’t know what’s up or down. Am I correct?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do they?

Depends on your headcannon. They could also come from SCP-2000 or basically anywhere your brain tells you they come from.

There Is No Canon, so there's no hard and fast rule of where anything comes from, or is. Just general concepts.

Also, the Monthly Termination thing is mostly a deprecated trope at this point, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You do realize that D-class come from prisons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then they'd get new D-class

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there was a monthly termination of D-class,then the foundation would run out of D-class in a year or 2.

item.title: I don't get it
item.content:encoded: This may be a stupid thing to say, but I don't really get things, specifically the end. What does the author imply?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Edit made, as I should have made the link months ago. Timer can run if anyone has further suggestions.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits have been made by the author as described below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so there are two locations here that I see as good potential crosslink spots. The first is the mention of D Lurk, whose name could certainly could be linked to a tale featuring the character. However, due to D. Lurk's presence in the AIAD canon, such a link would be a little redundant with the AIAD link already present. That aside, there is also a mention of Lunar Area-32, which can in turn be linked to that particular site dossier. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Personnel stationed at Lunar Area-32 reported no corresponding abnormalities at this time.

To this:

Personnel stationed at Lunar Area-32 reported no corresponding abnormalities at this time.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Second in after my split from the Foundation and we are coming in hot.

+ show block
– hide block

I find it a bit strange that everyone just assumes that the AI was right.
Functioning in normal parameters can mean anything to a computer.

I once wrote something similar, but in my case people started to doubt the AI before they believed that they themselves were affected.

It began the process of removing SCP-2193-1 throughout the Site's backup digital files, as well as every entry of the main database accessible by Site-17 personnel

When my AI starts deleting files I loudly shout: "Get away from the machine. Evacuate base. Initialise AI countermeasures."

Everyone just jumping to the conclusion that, since we cant find it any more (after an AI went full content filter on our database), therefore it's not real, is the real strangeness of the article.

QUESTION:"To comply with rule 2, who gave that AI clearance to delete stuff in the database?"
If deleting things for the benefit of the foundation is OK than the chance of an AI deleting entire Item files becomes plausible.
"To dangerous for humans to handle, to dangerous for Mankind to know."
Machines not alerting us to imminent danger since its better to die happy?

Is that what a LV 2 clearance dose?

Just Imagen that strange Moon video had not, conveniently, shown up going directly against the anomalies usual effect. We even have confirmation that the moon was inactive.
Where are the logs on how much ammo has been fired being kept? Computers way easier to manipulate than those highly secured databases?

I think some people have missed the terrifying implications.
I upvote for those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's just headcannon but I could have sworn they were not told and the official stance was if they survived until X they were set free.

I am not doubting your info. It makes the most sense here over the people arguing for D class terminations monthly. What I find genius is those arguing for it and downvoteing are affected by the skip so I upvoted even before coming here knowing what I would find. The skip lives here in the people who can't believe headcannon could be wrong. I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's never specifically stated in the writing guidelines, never mind that "there is no canon". This is just a commonly held assumption by lots of writers and readers.

Some earlier articles are written in such a way as suggests that the D-class know they will be terminated, but agree to work with the Foundation in exchange for an extra month to live and compensation to family members that they otherwise would not have received.

Other than that, the guide has at some point been updated to include a Class B or higher amnestitization OR termination "at the discretion of on-site security or medical staff". There's no mention of keeping their fate a secret.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "so horrible the Foundation don't mind losing them" bit is, more or less, spelled out in the writing guidelines - D-class are recruited from death row inmates who, in return for agreeing, are given a stay of execution for the duration of their term of service (Guidelines being just that, since there is canonically no canon). Being amnestitized rather than executed at the end of the month is just a better option given how few new death row inmates there are each year, and how long they sit there before being executed anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like your way of thinking. Personally, clones make sense when you have a granite bathtub in a canyon cave that makes copies of people that sleep in it. My headcanon is basically that D-classes ARE terminated monthly, just not all of them and termination means different things - fulfillment of commuted sentence (i.e., executed), amnestic reset (forget everything for the previous month while continuing to be utilized), or in specific rare cases where extranormal interrogation proves sufficient rehabilitation and loyalty, recruited as level-0 personnel. Any of those things can be considered termination because it means their contract as D-class has been terminated (which, I believe, is mentioned in the guide's outline on D-class recruitment) - especially since that ominous extra bit about recruiting from lesser crimes or even civilian populations is mentioned. It also makes those old mentions of demotion to D-class less harsh if they're moved to Safe-object handling.

item.title: Re: this is the stupidest thing ever
item.content:encoded:
When the procedure was first written back when the site was getting started and the "how-to"s were originally implemented, it was a method of including human test subjects that could be used without bringing up to much outright maliciousness in favor of justifiable moral ambiguity, while also leaving a somewhat sinister loophole. The monthly termination, in this regard. was simply the commuted sentence being fulfilled.

However, from a logistical standpoint and the way current trends in politics, ethics, and various prison systems are going, there just aren't enough to go around. Fewer countries even allow a death penalty to begin with than just a decade ago. It would simply be unwise to eliminate them for the sake of security when amnestics exist. The US currently only has around 2000 inmates awaiting such sentences, and with ever last one of them enlisted as D-Class it would only give about .8 D-class personnel per object.

Even ignoring the more sympathetic reasons most people don't like it (they feel bad that D-class are treated as disposable, which is what the D stands for in the first place). it just doesn't make sense for a budgeted government-subsidized organization on the scale of the Foundation to throw away sources simply for being violent criminals when many of them would probably be repentant and cooperative, or at the very least manageable with amnestics and behavioral reprogramming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Affected Class-D are fully compliant and cooperative throughout the entirety of the process. It should be noted that the affected Class-D will still verbally assault and threaten nearby personnel.

Wait, so when ordinary personnel are infected they tell the Class-D's about it? I thought part of the "Monthly Termination" thing was that Class-D's were kept ignorant about it and fooled into thinking that they'd be released after the month was up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

Lolwut

Well i didn’t see that coming. Weird mixture of meta here. I’m not a gigantic fan overall, but it’s pretty clear that something’s underneath it all and knows enough about the Foundation to know D-Class exists, as well as being able to get into systems for the hypnotic/memetic effect. The creativity of the force implies that it’s not really sapient as we know it, I like that.

It reminds me vaguely of my own SCP-789, a really specialized ‘predator’. But of course, they're not at all related, at least how I understand 789 and this to work.

Interesting interpretation of monthly termination though. All I can see it as is this video but upwards. A+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's competently written, and as uncomfortable as the building "let's crosslink things because crosslinking" initiative among the staff makes me, I didn't mind the inclusion of AIAD. The explanation as to how this was uncovered actually made sense, so to me it was a decent use of the device.

Unfortunately, I have many of the same issues regarding dependency on the site's previous writing practices that I did with SCP-2959. To briefly recap, it feels strange to call something out as being silly, while providing a handwavey justification for it to be cited later (intentionally or no).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let me bring this footnote to your attention:

Hidden cameras sewn into the guards' uniforms have been intentionally obscured, including in cases where they have no foreknowledge its existence.

It doesn't shut the cameras off, it does however (with the exception of the incident) make the present staff complicit in covering everything up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, and I think the ending brings just the right amount of wierd. However, how was it recorded? The Description explicitly states that the effect shuts off all recording devices.

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll give an upvote because the space abduction delighted me, though I feel like this could be developed further. I mean, the SCP Foundation would likely worry a bit about D-Classes floating into the sky, as well as some follow-ups on their Agent disappearing. Unless you mean to work on that after the contest. It doesn't have to explain everything, but I'd imagine there's a better sweet spot for mystery without completely concealing the events transpiring.

item.title: Re: this is the stupidest thing ever
item.content:encoded: There is a contest about D-Class going on, which may be why this SCP was created.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To dogpile on to the discussion at hand…

The wiki has always held the phrase 'There is no canon' as pretty much the byline. Yes, this conflicts with most of the foundation writing, but half the point of the D-class contest is the fact that monthly terminations make no sense, and should be explained. That was the whole premise.

There's no retcon here, 'cause there's no con to ret. Every article is, within itself, the Prime Canon. Every article trumps every other article within itself. This skip says terminations are a skip. Therefore it is.

You are, of course, free to vote however you wish, but if preserving canon is your motivation, I think you might be off the mark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I started off hating this for the same reasons as a lot of other folks on this post—what do you mean monthly termination is a cognitohazard??? It seemed too big and too arrogant to retcon. And then I read the whole thing and I love it. It's amazing. Take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guards are Agents- it's basically the Foundation's default title for "personnel what do stuff".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You might say it…pulls you right out of the story?

Ahahahaha, excellent!

On an unrelated note, i just noticed you mention RFID implants ceasing to function. RFIDs are just passive devices that bounce back some info when they're pinged on a specific radio frequency. An RFID implant ceasing to function could just mean they walked 15m away from the scanner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
@shadyshopkeep I can touch this up post contest to address your concerns.

With regards to the break in the pattern, I'd just like to point out that it wouldn't merit an incident report if nothing out of the ordinary occurred.

And tapnek, he doesn't 'go all superman' he's similarly taken. It's not as random as it seems.

In lieu of an expository note at the end, I'd like to call a bit of attention to some important bits:

All the world's a stage, we are merely players

It should be noted that the affected Class-D will still verbally assault and threaten nearby personnel

Performers and portrayers

This is perfectly choreographed, despite the lack of planning/forewarning.

I can understand how distressing it can be to see the sudden differences in the addenda. It's strange when people act out of character. You might say it…pulls you right out of the story?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're certainly welcome to your own interpretations and headcanon, but bear in mind that the SCP Wiki thrives on making you question everything you thought you knew about the Foundation on a regular basis.

There's tons of articles that reverse or invert common tropes; if you look at this as one of those, it works better.

I dunno why there's been so much hostility in the comments about poking at the D-Class tropes. Must be memetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was with this until the D-Class rising at thousands of times the speed of sound and the Agent at the end going all Superman.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it until the end, which seemed unnecessarily over-the-top and appears to contradict other parts of the article. Why are agents transporting the D-class personnel when earlier it says that security guards are doing it? How do the other guards/agents explain the loss of Agent Ysson, especially when every other instance just caused the loss of D-class? Do they think they "terminated" him with their side-arms too? How do we even have camera footage of Ysson when the article explicitly says that all hidden cameras are intentionally obscured?

-1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea that the monthly termination of D-class is itself a kind of anomaly is not anything particularly groundbreaking. However, this does a competent job putting a relatively unique new spin on that trope, especially considering the final addendum in which the D-class are launched into SPACE!!!

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe you, but it still doesn't sit well with me. Coattail-riding or not, it's still an unnecessary complication and namedrop. Sorry mate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really hope it doesn't come off that way. It's inclusion is one part inspiration, one part integral to the containment of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Response to elitegamer10 (and others with similar complaints) rather than specifically this SCP:

+ show block
– hide block

There is a D-Class-themed contest going on right now that explicitly suggests that people ignore or re-imagine D-Clsss monthly terminations and death-row recruitment. If direct challenges to these tropes makes you uncomfortable, get ready for an unpleasant week.

In response to this SCP: I initially thought it was a little too direct, grandiose, and comprehensive; Taking credit for the source of real-world readership controversy and using an uber-powerful compulsion effect to make entire sites of people with different backgrounds behave in a specific way.

But the ending was just so damn weird, I bought it. +!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think downvotes for "Does not compute with headcanon" are just dumb. Just seeing a lot of that being griped in here. That said, I find myself falling into that trap too sometimes and try to judge accordingly. Food for thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but I believe there's a way to save the idea without scrubbing it away as some regrettable aspect of early-site culture.

So write one then! Things can exist on the site that contradict each other. Just because this exists doesn't make everything that came before it null and void. The beauty of this site is that different, contradictory ideas can all have a place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand the complaint that this skip conflicts with other previous skips… Isn't half the point of the "there is no canon" thing to allow different ideas that contradict each other to succeed if both ideas are good?

If you like the monthly termination idea and want to believe that that's what happens then that's your head-canon. We can all choose what to believe and what to disregard. Whether I choose to believe a piece is canon or not doesn't affect my enjoyment of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting and weird, but maybe a bit too weird. I definitely don't care for the explicit inclusion of AIAD stuff — it was an unnecessary complication that, accurately or not, comes off as namedropping for the sake of coattail-riding. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My own headcanon is that the D-class are recruited from people so horrible the Foundation doesn't have a problem with them dying in the name of maintaining humanity, and that the numbers of such people are sufficiently limited that while everyone is told that they're culled after a month (and they themselves are told they'll be released after a month), in fact they are amnesticized and believe they're starting each month anew. (And screw you, spellcheck, "amnesticized" is so a word!)

On the other hand, I like this, and am curious about what's up with the "moon" blinking. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't there a recent skip which explained the -Js on the Foundation's database as disinformation to hide one item? A neat and logical solution to a longstanding headscratcher.

There's a reason people make retcons. They're better than handwaving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite my playful retort up thread, I'm not actually trying to retcon or do away with any aspect of the wiki. I have perhaps the most fluid of headcannons on the site.

To me, they're both clones and culled inmates. The D's are both monthly terminated, and they're not. The Foundation simultaneously employs mages, and will not recognize magic. Everything is both possible, and isn't. The beauty of no canon.

Like it or not, but I'm not trying to force any one interpretation down the userbase's throat. This was purely done in the spirit of the contest, with nary an ulterior motive.

ETA: Meant to be a reply to Buttfranklin.

ETA 2: Also, if the concept was 'scrubbed' from the Site altogether, this submission would lose relevancy, and be wholly pointless. I for one do not want it to disappear entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good tone, good structure. Can't fault you there, really. However, I'm going to no-vote because I'm fundamentally uncomfortable with the idea that we should retcon away the whole concept of monthly terminations. The concept is too deeply embedded into the site to just wish away under the pretence of some memetic effect. Obviously, I understand exactly why you'd want to do that, but I believe there's a way to save the idea without scrubbing it away as some regrettable aspect of early-site culture.

Edit: rather than reply to every mention of this comment elsewhere in the thread, I'll edit this one. I'm not uncompromisingly against the idea of articles on this site contradicting each other - generally, I don't give a hoot about it, at all. But to me, this isn't a matter of my headcanon, this is a matter of actual canon. The role of D-class as test subjects is referred to in, what, about half of all articles? The idea is mentioned so often, as is so fundamental to so many parts of the site, that, to me, it sets some definite limits on the wiggle room actively encouraged by this contest. From these contest entries, I'm expecting a re-imagining or a new perspective, sure, but one that still chimes with the huge preponderance of D-class-related content on-site. And I'd rather not have to take as a prerequisite that the entire Foundation is hopelessly cankered head-to-toe with some memetic infohazard. This entry (and others like it), with the overzealous retconning and excessive "everything you thought to be true was WRONG!" plot device, feel like someone trying way too hard to brush the concept of D-class testing and termination under the carpet because it's distasteful.

In short: the ideas are too established to mess with too much, so we just have to work around them, not chuck 'em in the garbage. I know this thinking might be a little rigid, but… think of me as a representative from the "annoying pedant" delegation on this site1. (I also actively believe that the immersiveness of the site improves with a certain level of canon-building, but that's a whole other debate.)

Footnotes
1. There are dozens of us! Dozens!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted, because this is really good, but SCP-2959, sort of covered the memetic D-Class angle really well. Unfortunately this was sort of spoiled for me.

Regardless, you did a really good job on this. I admire the writing here.
